text.title: Test Fixture Antipatterns
text.summary: Scope leakage, factory drift, Faker non-determinism, mock-as-fixture confusion, and cleanup race conditions.

antipattern.description: Fixture marked `scope="session"` or `scope="module"` when its state can be mutated by tests.
antipattern.reason: Mutable shared fixtures cause tests to pass or fail based on execution order. The failure is non-deterministic and only reproducible with the same test ordering. Default to `scope="function"`.

antipattern.description: Factory function defaults silently changed after 200 tests are written against the old defaults.
antipattern.reason: Factory defaults are the implicit contract for all tests that use the factory. Changing them without a type error breaks assumptions invisible to the test author. Use keyword-only args so all callers are explicit.

antipattern.description: `Faker('email')` used as the default for a field that appears in snapshot assertions.
antipattern.reason: Random Faker values change on every run, making snapshot tests flake. Use `factory.Sequence` or a fixed format like `f"user{n}@example.com"` for fields referenced in assertions.

antipattern.description: `Mock(spec=Service)` placed in `conftest.py` as a shared fixture without resetting between tests.
antipattern.reason: Mock call history accumulates across tests; `assert_called_with` checks from test N find calls from test N-1, producing false positives that mask real bugs.

antipattern.description: Yield-based fixture without try/finally when the cleanup can fail.
antipattern.reason: If the test raises during execution, the code after `yield` still runs. But if that cleanup code raises a second exception, it masks the original test failure, making diagnosis impossible.
`tmp_path` does not support session scope; pytest raises an obscure error. Use `tmp_path_factory.mktemp("...")` for session-scoped temporary directories.

antipattern.description: Returning a shared mutable list or dict from a factory function without deep-copying.
antipattern.reason: Test A mutates the returned object; Test B receives the same (mutated) object because factory returns the same reference. Always create new instances on each factory call.
